content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF

NIELSEN, MERKSAMER,
HODGSON, PARRINELLO & MUELLER
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION
770 L STREET, SUITE 800
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
TELEPHONE (916) 446-6752

MARIN COUNTY
591 REDWOOD HIGHWAY, #4000
MILL VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 94941
TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800

SAN FRANCISCO
650 CALIFORNIA STREET, SUITE 2650
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108
TELEPHONE (415) 989-6800

May 11, 1990

Jonathan Rothman, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Re: Confirmation of Informal Advice

Dear Mr. Rothman:

This letter will confirm informal advice provided by
you on May 2, 1990, regarding the general application of
Proposition 105 (Govt. Code §§ 84501-84514) to statewide
initiative campaigns.

A. Reporting Periods

1. We discussed the general application of Government
Code sections 84506 and 84511 to a statewide initiative campaign.
Since campaign statements filed in a new calendar year do not
carry over contributions received in the previous year by an
initiative campaign committee, the previous year's campaign
statements must be aggregated with the current year's campaign
statements for Proposition 105 disclosure purposes.

2. Section 84506 provides a seven day grace period
following the filing deadline of a campaign statement to modify
Proposition 105 disclosure as appropriate. For example, if
contributions received between March 18, 1990 and May 19, 1990
modify the major funding sources of an initiative committee,
their ads that previously identified sources based on January 1,
1989 through March 18, 1990 must be changed within seven days of
May 24, 1990 to identify sources based on January 1, 1989 through
May 19, 1990.

3. Late contribution notices (§84203) are not
considered "campaign finance disclosure reports" for calculating
cumulative contributions under §84507 to determine Proposition
105 disclosure. However, if an "optional" campaign statement is
filed pursuant to section 84511, that statement can be used to
make the Proposition 105 determination.

John Rothman, Esq.
May 11, 1990
Page 2


        4.     "Mandatory" amendments to campaign statements
(§81004.5) required by the PRA would be operative for Proposition
105 disclosure and would relate back to the campaign statement
for which the amendment was filed. Thus, an advertisement run
after an amendment was filed to a campaign statement should take
the amendment into consideration for purposes of Proposition 105
disclosure.

     B.    Required Disclosure

          We also discussed the application of the disclosure
provisions of Proposition 105, namely section 84507.

          1.     You confirmed that both section 84507(a) and (b)
require a single largest contributor and thus, if more than one
industry or person tied as the largest contributors, no
disclosure was required.

          2.     You confirmed that it was permissible for a
committee to return a contribution to a contributor even if the
resulting effect was a change in the Proposition 105 disclosure.
You suggested that I review the Elections Code for the propriety
of such a transaction but that such a transaction was not
inconsistent with the PRA, Proposition 105 or the personal use
law.

          3.     We also discussed the ability of a committee to
obtain additional contributions from previous contributors to
create a tie in the largest contributor category. It was your
opinion that such activity could create an "acting in concert"
situation which would compel disclosure of all the individuals
who tied as the largest contributor. That determination,
however, would necessarily be based on the facts of each specific
situation.

          However, you did believe it would not be improper for a
committee to establish a maximum contribution amount even if the
intended purpose was to facilitate a tie in the largest
contributor category.

John Rothman, Esq.
May 11, 1990
Page 3


     4.     Concerning the disclosure of corporations or
unions, you had no problem with the qualifier "California
corporations" but had concerns about qualifiers such as "small
business corporations" and definitely would not accept qualifiers
like "honest corporations".

     5.     You informed me that nothing in Proposition 105
authorizes the Commission to require the disclosure of the major
donors to a major funding source.  For example, if Committee A is
the major funding source of Committee B under section 84507(b),
only the name of Committee A will be disclosed on Committee B's
initiative advertising.  The donors of Committee A are irrelevant
except as they apply to determining sponsorship of a committee
(§82048.7).

     If I have misstated your conclusions please inform me
as soon as possible.  I look forward to receiving your written
confirmation.  As always, your courtesy and cooperation are
greatly appreciated.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        THOMAS W. HILTACHK


TWH/bg
